name: Katrina Lake
title: Katrina Lake Turned a Spreadsheet Into Your Stylist
dek: She built a fashion company out of data, hustle, and the radical idea that a robot and a human stylist could dress you better than you dress yourself.
body_html:
Most fashion empires start with a runway. Katrina Lake's started with an Excel sheet and a pile of clothes she was personally shipping out of her apartment. The pitch sounded almost too simple: what if an algorithm and a real human stylist teamed up to mail you clothes you'd actually keep? That idea became Stitch Fix, and it made Lake one of the youngest women ever to take a company public.

The Come-Up: Hustle in a Cardboard Box

Lake launched the concept around 2011 while at business school, originally under a different name, working out of her own home and literally buying inventory on credit cards to test whether anyone wanted this. The model was genuinely new: customers filled out a deep style quiz, paid a styling fee, and received a curated "Fix" of five items. Keep what you love, send back the rest.

Investors were skeptical, a fashion company that needed warehouses, inventory, and data scientists? Lake heard a lot of nos. She's been candid that fundraising as a young female founder in a male-dominated VC world meant fighting twice as hard for half the faith. She kept the receipts and kept building.

The Creativity: Machine Learning Meets Mom-Knows-Best

The brilliance of Stitch Fix wasn't the clothes. It was the marriage of data and human taste. Every Fix fed the system more signals, what you kept, what you returned, what you said you hated. Over time the algorithm learned your size quirks and secret preferences, then handed that intelligence to a human stylist who added the gut-feel a machine can't fake.

It flipped retail inside out. Instead of you scrolling endlessly through a thousand products, the product came to you, pre-filtered by a brain that knew your body and your vibe. The unboxing felt personal, a little note from your stylist, pieces chosen for you. That experience turned a transaction into a relationship, and the data turned that relationship into a moat competitors couldn't easily copy.

Anyone can sell clothes. Lake sold the feeling of being understood.

From Apartment Floor to the Bell at the Exchange

In 2017, Stitch Fix went public, and Lake reportedly became one of the youngest women to lead a company to IPO, famously ringing the bell while holding her young son on her hip, an image that launched a thousand "you can do both" think-pieces. The company grew to serve millions of clients and pull in revenue reported in the hundreds of millions and beyond, with Lake's own stake reportedly making her a multimillionaire on paper.

The journey hasn't been a straight rocket, like much of fashion tech, the business has weathered tough markets and shifting consumer habits, and Lake eventually stepped back from the CEO role she'd held for years. But the blueprint she drew is everywhere now: personalization, recommendation engines, the "we know what you want" promise baked into modern shopping.

She's since moved into investing and backing other founders, especially women navigating the same skeptical rooms she did. Because the real product Katrina Lake built wasn't just a styling service, it was proof that a young woman with a spreadsheet and stubborn conviction could outsmart an entire industry.

The kicker: She didn't disrupt fashion with a hot new look. She did it with a quiz, an algorithm, and a cardboard box.
